name: Grecotipo
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a grotesque (grotesca) by the foundry, with rounded internal counters and a variable structure spanning weight, italic and corner-rounding axes.
summary: A variable grotesque by Rodrigo Saiani and Carlos Mignot, designed for Greco, with three interpolation axes covering weight, italic and corner rounding.
description: Built as a commission for the studio Greco, Grecotipo comes in both static and variable formats, the latter spanning weight, slant and the degree of corner rounding across Text and Display optical sizes. At small sizes the design holds its functional restraint; at large sizes the rounded counters and vivid curves become the dominant characteristic. The family covers 32 static styles across eight weights with matching italics in both Text and Display cuts.
foundry_blurb: Plau describe Grecotipo as a typeface whose letterforms blend the physical and the digital, reflecting the way Greco approaches design. As a variable font it moves across three axes — weight, italics and corner-rounding intensity — shifting from contained and sober at small sizes to almost sculptural at large sizes, with each character designed to invite the eye in.

name: Grama
classification_notes: Grama is built from the geometry of football pitch markings, with perfectly circular letterforms, generous x-height and ink traps that give it a precise geometric structure typical of geometric sans-serifs.
summary: A geometric sans designed by Carlos Mignot, drawing its forms from the circles and boundary lines of a football pitch.
description: Designed by Carlos Mignot for Plau, the typeface translates football field markings into a typographic system of eight weights, each with a matching italic, plus a variable font version. Perfectly circular letters, generous x-height and distinctive ink traps give it a strong, consistent rhythm across weights. Aimed at sports branding, campaign work and identity design, it sidesteps obvious footballing clichés by grounding its character in geometric precision rather than iconography.
foundry_blurb: Plau describe Grama as a typeface inspired by the geometry of the football pitch, designed by Carlos Mignot. It transforms field markings, circles and penalty areas into a contemporary typographic system combining geometric precision with sporting energy. Eight carefully crafted weights range from the elegance of lighter styles to the bold expressiveness of the heaviest, with generous forms, perfectly circular letters and distinctive ink traps creating a strong and versatile personality suited to branding, sports identities, uniforms and campaigns.

name: Momento
classification_notes: Momento blends broad-nib calligraphic contrast and expressive serifs with geometric forms derived from near-perfect circles and sharp angles, placing it firmly in the high-contrast display serif category rather than a conventional text serif tradition.
summary: A high-contrast display serif from Brazilian designer Dan Schunck that fuses broad-nib calligraphic structure with near-geometric circular forms across eight weights.
description: The design tension comes from pairing the angular, 30-degree angled counters and expressive serifs of broad-nib calligraphy against almost perfectly circular bowls and sharp geometric proportions. Available as both static and variable font files, the family spans Thin to Black with 488 glyphs covering 38 languages, plus several alternate letter sets. Intended for large sizes from 16pt upwards, it suits headlines, book covers, logotypes, packaging and poster work where typographic presence is the point.
foundry_blurb: Plau describe Momento as a contemporary display typeface that combines two opposing worlds: the gestural quality of broad-nib calligraphy, with its high contrast, expressive serifs and 30-degree angled counters, and the simplicity of geometry drawn from nearly perfect circles, sharp angles and balanced proportions. A variable font with eight styles from Thin to Black, it covers 38 languages across 488 characters and includes alternate letter sets, designed to excel in titles, logos, packaging, book covers, campaigns and posters.

name: Spalla
classification_notes: An all-caps display typeface with classical proportions and Italian Art Deco references, designed exclusively for large-scale, high-impact use rather than continuous text.
summary: An all-caps display family in nine weights plus a variable version, drawing on classical proportions and Italian Art Deco references for editorial, packaging and branding work.
description: Named after the Italian word for shoulder — the supporting lead in opera and theatre — Spalla is built around architectural letterforms with a performative character. Its nine static weights run from Thin to Black, with a variable axis spanning the full range, and over 290 glyphs cover 27 languages. Opentype features include discretionary ligatures, several stylistic alternates and a full suite of numeral forms.
foundry_blurb: Plau describe Spalla as a typeface that carries the weight of tradition on its shoulders and the lightness of style, evoking golden café signs in Milan and vintage film posters in Rome. They position it for projects that need a strong voice with poise, suited to editorial, packaging, branding and shopfront applications.

name: Histerica
classification_notes: A display serif with organic, snail-shaped terminals and rounded-base serifs, revived from an early 20th-century French newspaper typeface and developed into a contemporary editorial family.
summary: A display serif drawn from a 1908 French newspaper typeface, developed by Ana Laura Ferraz into a five-weight family with curled terminals and rounded serifs, conceived as a comment on female rage and suffragette history.
description: Beginning as a revival of a typeface spotted in a 1908 edition of Le Petit Journal, Histerica grew into something deliberately contradictory: firm, robust uppercase letters paired with friendly lowercase forms, curled snail-like terminals alongside sharply cut corners. The five static weights from Light to ExtraBold are also available as a variable font, and the family is intended for book covers, posters, editorial titles and visual identities. Its conceptual framing, as a well-behaved form that still speaks to female anger, gives it a particular charge for feminist-adjacent editorial work.
foundry_blurb: Plau describe Histerica as inspired by a typeface in the 1908 edition of Le Petit Journal, where a charming, snail-terminated serif seemed at odds with the seriousness of the suffragette news it was setting. Designed by Ana Laura Ferraz, what began as a revival gained new weights and characteristics, arriving at a result with friendly lowercase letters and firm, robust uppercase forms — a font that, despite being well-behaved, asserts itself as a tool for giving voice to female rage.

name: Ana Banana
classification_notes: A slightly condensed Latin sans-serif with humanist warmth expressed through bell-shaped stroke terminals, deep inktraps and 'smiling' counters, functional across text and display use.
summary: A variable Latin sans by Júlia Lago with deep inktraps, bell-shaped terminals and smiling counters, spanning nine weights from Hairline to Black.
description: Developed in 2021 at Studio Motora and released through Plau, Ana Banana carries a distinctly cheerful character through its bell-shaped stroke endings and open, upward-curving counters, without sacrificing text-setting utility. Its slightly condensed proportions make the lighter weights well-suited to running copy, while the Black is expressive enough for poster and identity work. The family won a Platinum at the 2022 CLAP Awards for best type design for text and was shortlisted at Tipos Latinos.
foundry_blurb: Plau describe Ana Banana as a typeface developed in 2021 and tested across social media, catalogues, print, apparel and contract fine print before its commercial release. A variable font with nine weights, it features deep inktraps, bell-shaped stroke endings and smiling counters, producing a cheerful and varied family that retains functionality and unity across all communication.

name: Eclisse
classification_notes: Explicitly described as a hyper display serif drawing from the Didone tradition, with razor-thin serifs and high contrast characteristic of that style, optimised entirely for large-scale display use.
summary: A high-contrast Didone display serif by Brazilian designers Carlos Mignot and Nelson Balaban, built for luxury identities, editorial headlines and logotypes.
description: Drawn in five weights from ExtraLight to Bold with matching italics, Eclisse takes Didone as its structural reference but widens circular letterforms beyond classical convention to create a distinctive silhouette at large sizes. Razor-thin serifs and strong stroke contrast make it suited to fashion editorials, book covers, premium packaging and identity work. A variable font version is included alongside the static styles.
foundry_blurb: Plau describe Eclisse as an ode to distinction, created by Nelson Balaban and Carlos Mignot in an unprecedented collaboration. They position it as a hyper display serif with five weights, inspired by the Didone style but with deliberately wider circular letters and razor-thin serifs, accompanied by a rich set of alternative characters for logos and visual compositions.

name: Carbona
classification_notes: Carbona is a geometric sans-serif with a futurist, technology-oriented character, available in both proportional and monospaced variants with a variable font axis spanning weight and slant.
summary: A geometric sans from Brazilian foundry Plau, built for digital interfaces and technology contexts, with a full monospaced subfamily and cryptocurrency symbol support.
description: Designed by Carlos Mignot, Carbona spans eight weights from ExtraLight to Black in both proportional and monospaced versions, each with slanted counterparts, and is also available as a variable font. The family includes cryptocurrency symbols, advanced OpenType features for interface use, and programming optimisations in the Mono subfamily. Its alternates include a high-contrast set and sharp-edge variants, giving designers control over the typeface's character across dashboards, fintech branding and editorial displays.
foundry_blurb: Plau describe Carbona as their attempt to imagine the future through typography, conceived as a typeface that converses with everything on the technological horizon — from new digital currencies to 5G data transmission. The family is designed to handle the demands of the digital world today and in what's still to come, optimised for programming, loaded with cryptocurrency symbols and advanced interface features, and suited to use across interfaces, dashboards, apps, terminals, data systems and fintech branding.

name: Amarta
classification_notes: A sans-serif with narrow counters and width variation in its Poster subfamily, drawing on classic sports typography vernacular with a disciplined, structured character suited to both editorial text and display headlines.
summary: A dual-optical sans family designed for sports contexts, named in tribute to Brazilian football player Marta Silva, with separate Text and Poster subfamilies each optimised for their intended scale.
description: Designed by Diego Maldonado at Plau, the family draws on classic typefaces from sports visual identity and splits into two distinct but related subfamilies. Amarta Text covers paragraph and mid-size use with weight and slant variable axes, while Amarta Poster targets large headlines with narrow counters and a width axis spanning Condensed, Narrow and Regular. Both families are available as static styles and as variable fonts, with OpenType features including built-in accents for blocked titles, vertical fractions and alternate characters.
foundry_blurb: Plau describe Amarta as inspired by classic typefaces used in sports visuals, offering two size-specific subfamilies that share the same spirit but differ in design and intent. The Text subfamily suits running text and mid-size settings via weight and slant variation, while the Poster subfamily features narrow counters and width variation for high-impact title use. The name honours Marta Silva, one of the greatest athletes in Brazilian history.

name: Redonda
classification_notes: Described by Plau as a humanist sans that evolved from a geometric starting point; the dual personality between humanist warmth and geometric aspiration places it firmly in the humanist sans subcategory with geometric leanings.
summary: A Brazilian humanist sans by Carlos Mignot that began life as a heavy geometric display face before expanding into a full, wide-ranging family with condensed and compressed widths.
description: Designed originally as a custom face for Plau's own branding, Redonda grew into a 36-style retail release spanning Light to Black across standard, condensed and compressed widths, each with matching italics. A variable font is also included, offering continuous control over weight, width and slant. Alternates for geometric and humanist forms of key letters such as 'a', 'g' and 'c' allow the typeface to shift character depending on context, from compact logotypes to body copy in UI.
foundry_blurb: Plau describe Redonda as humanist on one hand and geometric wannabe on the other. Born from a desire for an exclusive typeface for their own communication, it grew too large to keep in-house and became Plau's tenth retail family and the first signed by Carlos Mignot. The design began as a Black-only uppercase geometric face and developed into a versatile humanist sans capable of performance in interfaces and running text as well as high-impact headlines.

name: Firula
classification_notes: Firula is a variable connected script typeface with brush-drawn letterforms, swashes, cursive capitals and contextual ligatures, placing it firmly in the connected script category with a calligraphic brush influence.
summary: A variable connected brush script in seven weights, designed by Rodrigo Saiani for Plau, with cursive capitals, swashes and contextual ligatures intended for logos, posters and event materials.
description: Built as a fully variable font with seven named stops from Light to Black, Firula simulates the rhythm of a brush-drawn hand through connected letterforms and a suite of OpenType features including contextual alternates, swashes, St ligatures and an alternate final t. Lighter weights carry enough legibility for short phrases; heavier cuts suit single words and display headlines where the cursive capitals and swashes come into their own. Glyph coverage spans Latin Extended with fractions, super- and subscripts, and case-sensitive forms.
foundry_blurb: Plau describe Firula as flourish, ornament and embellishment — a connected variable script in seven styles that embraces joy. They suggest using it for logos, labels, posters, book covers, post titles and invitations, particularly for music, gastronomy and cultural events, with lighter weights for longer phrases and heavier weights for short, attention-grabbing words.

name: Massima
classification_notes: Massima is explicitly modelled on Futura, a canonical geometric sans, retaining its circular forms and clean structure in lighter weights while introducing more expressive details at heavier weights, including diagonal terminals in the Ultra cut.
summary: A Futura-derived geometric sans by Carlos Mignot, spanning ten weights with italics and a variable format, designed to shift from neutral text workhorse to expressive display face as weight increases.
description: Designed by Carlos Mignot and published by Plau, Massima takes Futura as its starting point but develops its own character across the weight range. Lighter cuts behave as a clean, balanced geometric suited to text, UI and signage, while heavier weights introduce diagonal terminals and more pronounced forms, culminating in the Ultra, which reads as a distinct display face. Short ascenders and descenders make it well suited to tight, compact headline setting. A Massima Text subfamily and variable format are included in the family.
foundry_blurb: Plau describe Massima as a typeface that revives the timeless spirit of Futura, created by Carlos Mignot as an homage to 20th-century modern design reinterpreted for the present. They note that while lighter weights retain the balance and simplicity of a geometric sans, the heavier weights gain personality, with the Ultra featuring diagonal terminals and expressive forms. Short ascenders and descenders optimise it for compact, impactful headline setting across identities, product branding, digital interfaces, signage, editorial and presentation contexts.

name: Pacaembu
classification_notes: A sans-serif rooted in the stone-cut lettering of a 1940 Art Deco stadium in São Paulo; the geometric, decorative character of those carved letters places it firmly in the Art Deco sans tradition.
summary: A seven-weight Art Deco sans drawn from the stone-cut lettering of the Pacaembu Stadium in São Paulo, celebrating the visual culture of Brazilian football.
description: Designed by Álvaro Franca and Felipe Casaprima, the family began as a study of the carved letterforms found at the Municipal Stadium Paulo Machado de Carvalho, an Art Deco landmark inaugurated in 1940. Those European-origin letters took on a distinctly Brazilian character over decades of football culture, and that personality is carried through seven weights from Thin to Ultra, with a variable font also available. OpenType features include a one-storey 'a', a short J, proportional figures and an alternative question mark, making it well-suited to identities, posters, signage and editorial work.
foundry_blurb: Plau describe Pacaembu as a sans serif typeface rooted in Brazilian football, begun as a study of the stone-cut Art Deco lettering at Pacaembu Stadium, inaugurated in 1940. They note that Art Deco letterforms, like football itself, were brought to Brazil by Europeans but found a wholly unique personality in the tropics. The family is positioned for identities, book covers, posters, packaging, signage, editorial and digital projects, with its Art Deco flavour lending itself to headlines, logotypes and sports or urban culture communications.

name: Matria
classification_notes: A grotesque family with historical Brazilian references, featuring high-waist capitals and increasing stroke contrast at heavier weights, consistent with the grotesque tradition rather than geometric or humanist sans.
summary: A grotesque family by Rodrigo Saiani rooted in a 1924 Brazilian type specimen, spanning four widths and nine weights with full italic coverage and variable font technology.
description: The design stems from a condensed heavy face discovered in a mid-1924 Brazilian printing office specimen, loosely related to Reform Grotesk from Stempel but with high-waist capitals, a distinctive uppercase R and a mild stroke contrast that intensifies towards the heavier weights. Four widths run from Compressed to Expanded, each with nine weights and matching italics, all unified in a single variable font. Alternate forms for lowercase a, g and l, plus an alternate uppercase J, allow shifts in tone between the technical and the warm.
foundry_blurb: Plau describe Matria as a grotesque family based on Brazilian historical models, discovered while working with a centennial client whose 1924 type specimens contained a condensed heavy face used on health, beauty and pharmaceutical labels. Their interpretation targets contemporary branding and editorial use, with contrast that increases as weights grow heavier, four widths with infinite in-between variations via variable font technology, and alternates for a, g, l and J. Tried and tested across coffee packaging, hotel chains and fashion companies.

name: Paddock
classification_notes: A display-oriented geometric sans with strong width variation and a futuristic, speed-influenced aesthetic; the oblique/slanted variants and motorsport-derived curves push it firmly into display territory rather than neutral text use.
summary: A motorsport-inspired display family by Carlos Mignot spanning five widths and six weights, with slanted variants and a variable font edition.
description: Drawn with direct reference to high-speed automotive culture, the family runs from UltraCondensed to Wide, giving designers a wide range of hierarchical options within a single consistent voice. Inner curves are more tightly defined against fluid, open outer curves, producing a sense of forward motion at any width. Suited to sports branding, event posters, broadcast graphics and motion design contexts where expressive width variation is needed.
foundry_blurb: Plau describe Paddock as designed to accelerate, capturing the essence of speed through five widths and six styles plus italic variants. Inspired by the high-speed automotive world, the contrast between defined inner curves and fluid outer curves is said to evoke the adrenaline of the racetrack. Plau recommend it for technology and motorsport identities, magazine covers, event posters, broadcast panels, motion design and real-time data interfaces.

name: Realce
classification_notes: Rooted in grotesque sans-serif traditions but ultra-condensed with a blocky, high-impact structure designed explicitly for display use; the extreme proportions and tall x-height place it firmly in the display category.
summary: An ultra-condensed grotesque display typeface by Carlos Mignot, built for high-density headlines and bold visual communication.
description: Designed by Carlos Mignot and released by Brazilian foundry Plau, Realce pairs an ultra-condensed, blocky structure with a generous x-height so that lowercase letters carry equal visual weight to uppercase. Low-profile diacritical marks enable tighter line spacing without sacrificing readability, while micro-rounded corners temper the otherwise hard-edged geometry. The family ships in six static weights from ExtraLight to Black, plus a variable font counterpart covering the same range.
foundry_blurb: Plau describe Realce as a bold, high-impact typeface rooted in grotesque sans-serif traditions, featuring an ultra-condensed and blocky structure built to command attention. Its tall x-height makes lowercase as prominent as uppercase, low-profile accents allow tighter line spacing, and subtle micro-rounded corners add refinement. The foundry position it as a go-to display font for striking headlines, bold branding and standout posters.

name: Quiz
classification_notes: Quiz draws from calligraphic tradition, giving it the organic stroke contrast and hand-finished detail characteristic of humanist sans-serifs, while remaining functional across both text and display settings.
summary: A calligraphically inspired humanist sans by Lu Leitenperger, offered in 28 styles split across text and display optical sizes with seven weights each.
description: Designed by Lu Leitenperger and engineered by Henrique Beier, Quiz takes cues from calligraphy to produce a sans-serif with enough personality to stand apart from neutral grotesques. The family spans Regular through ExtraBlack in both Text and Display optical sizes, with italics that bring out its hand-finished character more explicitly. Variable font and Office versions extend its reach across screen, print, identity and editorial contexts.
foundry_blurb: Plau describe Quiz as expressive without sacrificing functionality, suited to long-form text and impactful headlines alike. Designed by Lu Leitenperger with calligraphic inspiration, it arrives in 28 styles covering text and display versions across seven weights, with italics that reveal its hand-finished quality. Plau position it as a creative alternative to neutral sans-serifs, recommended for brand identities, editorial systems, book and magazine covers, packaging and point-of-sale materials.

name: Guanabara
classification_notes: A humanist sans with calligraphic terminal details, classic capital proportions and a tapered R leg that give it warmth and personality beyond a neutral grotesque.
summary: A Rio de Janeiro-inspired humanist sans by Rodrigo Saiani, originally conceived as a wayfinding typeface, grown into a full working family spanning eight weights with a companion display subfamily.
description: Designed around the curves and character of Rio de Janeiro, Guanabara runs from Thin to Black across both static and variable font formats, with true italics throughout the Sans subfamily. Distinctive details include a tapered R leg, calligraphic terminal endings and stylistic alternates for /a, /g and /r, complemented by small caps, oldstyle figures and tabular numerals. The Display subfamily strips back texture for headline use, letting the letterforms' elegance carry at large sizes.
foundry_blurb: Plau describe Guanabara as their third ever release, born from a brief to create a wayfinding typeface with enough personality to serve as the brand typeface for Rio de Janeiro. The city's exuberant curves and year-round summer provided the design constraints, and the family grew from there into what they call the 'Typeface from Ipanema': classic proportions, calligraphic finishes and a joyful, swinging elegance that welcomes many cultures and scripts.

name: Juma
classification_notes: Juma is an OpenType color font designed exclusively for large display use, with no text weights or optical sizes; its decorative, sculptural letterforms and colour variants place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A super-display OpenType colour font by Brazilian lettering artist Cyla Costa, with swirling organic forms evoking tropical birds and marbled stone.
description: Designed by Cyla Costa and produced by Álvaro Franca and Felipe Casaprima, Juma comes in two styles: a full-colour version and a monochrome version, each with OpenType alternates for specific letterforms in distinct colour palettes. The letterforms carry a sculptural weight that works as decorated drop caps, headline type or abstract pattern work, always at large sizes. It won Gold at the Latin American Design Awards 2022 and the Communication Arts Typography Annual 2022.
foundry_blurb: Plau describe Juma as a super display colour font made to be used huge, created by Brazilian lettering artist Cyla Costa as a deep exploration of shapes to push the boundaries of her visual language. Its forms and colours evoke tropical birds and marble, with an almost hypnotic quality; on the page it suits drop caps, striking headlines and abstract compositions, while each individual letter has a sculptural quality that invites you to imagine it as an object.

name: Kimura Sans
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a humanist typeface by the foundry, with organic stroke contrast and flexibility across text and display settings typical of the humanist sans genre.
summary: A humanist sans from Brazilian foundry Plau, designed by Carlos Mignot and offered in text and display subfamilies with a variable font companion.
description: Designed by Carlos Mignot at Plau and inspired by the work of Marcelo Kimura, the family spans seven weights from Thin to Black with matching italics across two optical subfamilies: Kimura Sans for text and interface use, and Kimura Sans Display for headlines and campaigns where bolder gestures are needed. A variable font version is also available, offering axes for weight, optical size and slant. The family is positioned for visual identity systems, editorial layouts, digital interfaces and brand campaigns.
foundry_blurb: Plau describe Kimura Sans as a versatile humanist typeface created by Carlos Mignot, inspired by the work of Marcelo Kimura. They position it for visual identities, brand systems, presentations, websites, apps and editorial materials, with the Sans subfamily handling text and interface work and the Sans Display subfamily taking on headlines, covers and campaigns where expressive weights and gestures need to come forward.

name: Compasso
classification_notes: Compasso is explicitly rooted in the geometric sans tradition, drawing from Futura, Avenir and the Ludlow Tempo, with optical adjustments made throughout to soften the rigidity of pure geometry.
summary: A geometric sans by Rodrigo Saiani that spans nine weights and three widths, with optical refinements that soften its mathematical foundations into something more rhythmic.
description: Designed by Rodrigo Saiani at Plau, Compasso takes the geometric sans tradition of Futura and Avenir as its starting point, then adjusts and loosens those forms for practical use across brand identities, editorial systems, signage and digital interfaces. The family runs from Thin to Black across normal, Condensed and Extended widths, each with matching italics, and is also available as a variable font covering both weight and width axes. Alternate glyphs including a single-storey 'a', a barless 'G' and a double-storey italic 'g' give further stylistic control.
foundry_blurb: Plau describe Compasso as the result of designer Rodrigo Saiani's growth in typographic understanding, moving from a reverence for geometric precision towards a deeper appreciation of optical correction and visual rhythm. Informed by Futura, Avenir, Tempo and contemporary sans-serifs including Mallory and Azo Sans, the family expanded from its original core to include condensed and extended widths. A contribution from designer Nicole Rauen reframed the project entirely: Compasso, they say, is not about geometry but about rhythm.

name: Tenez
classification_notes: Tenez is a high-contrast display didone rooted in pointed nib calligraphy, drawing on Bodoni and Didot influences while maintaining deliberately organic construction that echoes late 19th-century advertising typography.
summary: A display didone from Plau, rooted in pointed nib calligraphy and designed with deliberately organic construction for contemporary branding and editorial use.
description: Designed by Rodrigo Saiani and originating from a client branding project, Tenez takes its cues from Bodoni and Didot but resists the rigidity of strict neoclassical revival by favouring organic, hand-cut character. The family covers four weights from Light to Black, each with italics, and is also available as a variable font spanning nine weight steps from Thin to Black in both roman and italic axes. It is suited to large-scale applications such as packaging, editorial covers and identity work where high contrast and calligraphic detail can be appreciated.
foundry_blurb: Plau describe Tenez as a Grand Slam display didone, born from a branding project and developed into a full typeface intended to meet broad designer needs. Its forms originate in pointed nib calligraphy, referencing the hand-cut origins of typefaces like Bodoni and Didot, with an explicit design challenge to keep the construction as organic as possible. They position it for display use in branding, covers, packaging and editorial headings, where its italics and alternates can take centre stage.

name: Modet
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a humanist sans-serif by the foundry, with open letterforms, a double-storey 'a' and 'g' as defaults, and a range of alternates that shift character from neutral to expressive.
summary: A humanist sans-serif from Brazilian foundry Plau, spanning ten roman weights from Thin to UltraBlack with an extensive set of OpenType alternates for tonal flexibility.
description: Designed by Matheus Fragoso, Modet covers 289 languages across ten static weights and a variable font, making it a practical choice for international editorial and identity work. Its default letterforms are warm and legible at small sizes, while alternates for the 'a', 'g', 't', 'K', 'R' and ampersand allow the typographer to shift the voice from neutral to more distinctive. The range of OpenType features includes square punctuation, serifed 'I', curved leg variants and multiple ampersand styles, giving designers meaningful control over personality.
foundry_blurb: Plau describe Modet as a versatile and friendly humanist sans-serif suited to any typographic task, readable at small sizes and characterful at display sizes. It comes in 10 roman styles from thin to Ultra Black, speaks 289 languages, and can be reshaped through its many alternate characters and OpenType features, making it a strong performer for editorial and branding projects.

name: Dupincel
classification_notes: Dupincel is a text serif with optical size variants and expressive italics, positioned for long-form editorial and literary work, consistent with a transitional or contemporary text serif tradition.
summary: A text serif by Rodrigo Saiani built around three optical sizes and a full set of expressive OpenType features, designed for long-form editorial and literary publishing.
description: Seven years in development, Dupincel covers Small, Medium and Large optical sizes across six weights from Light to Black, each with a matching italic, plus variable font versions of the complete range. OpenType features include swashes, small capitals, oldstyle figures, ligatures and a historical italic 'h', giving the family real depth for book, magazine and identity work. A TDC Certificate of Excellence winner, available via Adobe Fonts and Type Network.
foundry_blurb: Plau describe Dupincel as a typeface made for storytelling, drawn by Rodrigo Saiani over seven years. Three optical sizes — Small, Medium and Large — optimise it from running text to display headings, while swashes, small capitals, oldstyle figures and ligatures give designers the resources to tell any kind of story. Plau suggest it for short-story collections, novels, magazines, long reports and law firm identities.

name: Manteiga
classification_notes: Manteiga is a brush lettering-derived script with deliberate hand-made inconsistencies, ultra-tight spacing and a short x-height, placing it firmly in the expressive brush script category.
summary: A single-weight brush script by Rodrigo Saiani, designed for large-scale display use with deliberately hand-drawn inconsistencies and ultra-tight spacing.
description: Named after the Portuguese word for butter, Manteiga takes brush lettering as its starting point and pushes the letterforms as far as possible while maintaining legible positive-to-negative space balance. The intentionally short x-height and tight spacing give it a quirky, textured voice suited to headlines, packaging and logotypes rather than body text. OpenType features include alternate Q, a and g shapes, a creamy tilde variant, stylistic sets and a handful of dingbats including a cow and assorted hand symbols.
foundry_blurb: Plau describe Manteiga as a typeface for heart-melting, word-spreading goodness, inspired by Julia Child's maxim that great French cooking requires butter, butter, butter. The design plays with brush lettering to push letterform shapes to their limits while balancing positive and negative space, with small deliberate inconsistencies that add texture and life. Plau kept OpenType features purposeful rather than extensive, and recommend using Manteiga large, never small.

name: Vinila
classification_notes: Vinila is a multi-width grotesque sans-serif with obliques rather than true italics, drawing on the genre's broad utility across text and display sizes. Its ink traps and rhythmic structure place it firmly within the grotesque tradition.
summary: A multi-width grotesque by Flora de Carvalho with pronounced ink traps, born from album lettering for a Brazilian music release.
description: Designed by Flora de Carvalho at Plau, the family spans four widths from Compressed to Extended, each with six weights and corresponding obliques, totalling 48 styles. Prominent ink traps shift character across the range, reading as delicate in lighter cuts and forceful in heavier ones, sustaining legibility from text to display. A variable font version offering continuous control over weight, width and slant is included with the complete family licence.
foundry_blurb: Plau describe Vinila as Flora de Carvalho's take on the grotesque genre, built for multi-purpose use across editorial, branding, signage and interfaces. The design originated as lettering for a Brazilian music album cover, with rhythm and music shaping its distinctive character. Its four widths, six weights per width and obliques are complemented by a variable font version available with the complete family, and the foundry notes they use it in their own brand identity daily.

name: Olivita
classification_notes: Olivita takes the bracket-serif, typewriter-genre conventions of ITC American Typewriter and pushes them into extreme fat-face weight territory, giving it a display slab character with deliberate inkiness and contorted counters.
summary: A single-weight fat-face riff on ITC American Typewriter by Rodrigo Saiani, pushed to an extreme heaviness that foregrounds dense, creamy texture at large sizes.
description: Designed by Rodrigo Saiani at Plau, Olivita takes the bracketed-serif conventions of the American Typewriter genre and inflates them to the point where counters contort and letterforms press against their limits. The tall x-height is balanced by long ascenders and descenders, and the ink-heavy texture is best appreciated at display sizes in short measures. Two styles, Regular and Italic, are offered, with the italic introducing cursive variants for r and s alongside a slanted single-storey a.
foundry_blurb: Plau describe Olivita as a fat-face interpretation of the American Typewriter genre, referencing ITC American Typewriter's influence on generations of designers and its appearance in Milton Glaser's I Heart NY logo. They ask what would happen if American Typewriter were inflated, and Olivita is their heavyweight answer, recommended for headlines, identities, packaging and display use, best set large with short lines to let its rich, creamy texture come forward.

name: Motiva Sans
classification_notes: A functional sans-serif designed for UI and digital environments, with cursive italics that suggest humanist influence; broad weight range and OpenType feature set place it firmly in the humanist sans tradition.
summary: The official typeface of Valve's Steam OS, designed by Rodrigo Saiani across seven weights with matching italics for a platform serving over 125 million users.
description: Built for high legibility across screen and print, it spans seven weights from Thin to Black with corresponding italics, all available as variable fonts. The italics are notably more cursive than typical sans-serif designs, providing strong contrast against the roman cuts. OpenType features include small caps, tabular and oldstyle figures, fractions, scientific inferiors and superiors, and alternate forms for the italic f and k.
foundry_blurb: Plau describe Motiva Sans as the official typeface of Steam, Valve's gaming platform with over 125 million users. With 14 fonts across seven weights and matching italics, it includes essential OpenType features and extended language support, performing well in both digital and printed contexts. The italics are more cursive than typical sans-serif designs, offering strong contrast to the roman styles.

name: Primot
classification_notes: Primot is explicitly described as an upright script with vertical connections drawn from brush lettering traditions, making it a display script with no slant, which distinguishes it from conventional cursive or calligraphic scripts.
summary: An upright display script by Rodrigo Saiani, drawn from vintage Italian gelateria lettering with brush flairs, vertical connections and over 900 glyphs.
description: Designed by Rodrigo Saiani at Plau, it departs from the conventional slanted script form in favour of a vertical orientation inspired by Italian gelateria signage and historical brush lettering. The family includes initial and final letterforms, contextual alternates and swashes to support expressive short-word composition in logos, packaging and signage. With over 900 glyphs and extended Latin coverage, it offers considerable flexibility for display work despite being a single-style release.
foundry_blurb: Plau describe Primot as a vertical upright script influenced by Italian gelaterias, developed after three sans-serif releases as an opportunity to work with fewer legibility constraints. They highlight unusual vertical letter connections and brush-influenced terminal flairs as defining features, and include hand-lettering-inspired OpenType features such as initial and final forms, contextual alternates and swashes, making it suitable for logos, video game titles, shop windows and similar display applications.

name: Odisseia
classification_notes: Odisseia is explicitly a monospaced typeface where every character occupies an identical horizontal unit, placing it squarely in the mono category with no meaningful subcategory distinction beyond that.
summary: A monospaced family from Plau with four weights, a generous x-height and a slightly shorter vertical scale than typical typewriter-derived fonts.
description: Designed by Rodrigo Saiani with assistance from Flora de Carvalho, Odisseia applies the fixed-width constraint across Light, Regular, Bold and Black weights, each requiring character-level adjustments to maintain consistent colour across the range. It sits shorter than most monospaced fonts and carries a large x-height, which gives it a compact, structured rhythm well suited to code editors, information systems and brand identities where vertical alignment and even texture matter. The family includes italics for all four weights and covers Latin Extended character sets including diacritics for Central and Eastern European languages.
foundry_blurb: Plau describe Odisseia as a foray into monospace territory, where every letter must occupy identical space regardless of its natural width. They note that this constraint required adapting individual character shapes across the four weights to preserve consistent light-to-dark balance throughout the family. They position it for uses ranging from computer coding to systemised brand communication, and highlight its shorter overall height and larger x-height as distinguishing it from other monospaced typefaces.

name: Plau
classification_notes: Described as futurist with rounded corners and deliberate lettershapes, placing it firmly in the geometric sans tradition with a contemporary, technology-inflected character.
summary: A rounded geometric sans by Rodrigo Saiani, designed with deliberately considered lettershapes for a technological yet approachable tone.
description: Designed by Rodrigo Saiani and available through Adobe Fonts via Type Network, Plau pairs rounded corners with unusually deliberate letterforms that sit comfortably in headlines while holding up across longer passages of text. The family spans twelve styles from Thin to Black with matching italics, and includes a strong set of OpenType features: small caps, fractions, proportional and tabular oldstyle and lining figures, and subscript and superscript numerals. Intended for identities, interfaces and editorial work where a friendly, contemporary and technological register is needed.
foundry_blurb: Plau describe the typeface as a futurist sans for the programming era, with rounded corners and interestingly deliberate lettershapes. They note it is comfortable in headlines and performs surprisingly well in longer text, and highlight its OpenType feature set including small caps, fractions, oldstyle numerals and superscript and subscript figures.

name: Salsero
classification_notes: Salsero is a single-style display serif with inverted contrast and rounded serifs, designed explicitly for headlines, packaging and identity work rather than text setting, placing it firmly in the display category.
summary: A single-style display serif by Rodrigo Saiani with inverted contrast and expressive curves, pitched squarely at headlines, packaging and short-form identity work.
description: Designed by Rodrigo Saiani at Plau, Salsero uses inverted stroke contrast alongside rounded serifs and deliberately playful curves to produce something warm, retro and distinctly Latin in character. It ships as one file with no weight axis or style variants, keeping the proposition simple: use it big, use it short, let the personality do the work. Available both to purchase directly and to sync via Adobe Fonts through Type Network.
foundry_blurb: Plau describe Salsero as a single-file display font with no style family ambitions, noting it has inverted contrast, expressive curves and intentional imperfections. Written in a mixture of Spanish, Portuguese and English, the copy positions it as suited to titles, packaging, posters and identities where graphic presence and retro flavour are needed, and highlights its multilingual Latin character set covering Portuguese, Spanish, German, French and other Western European languages.
